tate of Califorqia 
Fair Political Practices CommissioIl. 
P.o. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance/Administration 
Executive/Lena! 
••• 
Enforcement 
Conflict of Interes' 
(916) 3225660 
322·5901 
December 5, 1977 
Mr. Robert J. Logan 
City Attorney 
City of Pittsburg 
1901 Railroad Avenue 
Pittsburg, California 
94565 
Dear Mr. Logan: 
322·6441 
This is in response to your letter of October 3, 1977, 
to the Commission requesting an informal opinion concerning 
322·6444 
a situation involving Mr. Joseph Siino, a City Councilmember 
in the City of Pittsburg. 
Specifically you ask whether Mr. 
Siino may have been guilty of a violation of the conflict of 
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act when (and if) 
he participated in a decision to approve a project proposed 
by the Pittsburg Economic Housing and Development Corporation 
(hereinafter PEHDC) I a non-profit corporation responsible for 
the development and funding of economic projects within the 
City. 
Initially there appears to be some lack of clarity as 
to whether the project was, in fact, brought before the City 
Council for approval, and whether or not Mr. Siino was a 
member of the Council at the time it may have been consid-
ered. 
Since the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Political Reform Act relate only to actions taken by public 
officials in their official capacity, we will assume for 
the purposes of this advice letter that Mr. Siino did par-
ticipate in a decision to approve the project in question. 
Government Code SS87100 and 87103 provide in relevant 
part that: 
No public official at any level of state 
or local government shall make, participate 
in making or in any way attempt to use his 
official position to influence a governmental 
decision in which he knows or has reason to 
know he 
a financial interest. 


Robert J. Logan 
December 5, 1977 
Page Two 
An official has a financial interest in a 
decision within the meaning of §87100 if it 
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision 
will have a material financial effect, dis-
tinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally, on: 
(a) Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment 
worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000): 
. . . . 
(c) Any source of income, other than loans 
by a commercial lending institution in the 
regular course of business, aggregating two 
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in 
value received by or promised to the public 
official within twelve months prior to the 
time when the decision is made; ..•. 
87l03(a) and (c) 
The facts concerning the alleged conflict appear to be 
as follows. 
Mr. Siino is, and at all times has been, a prin-
cipal stockholder and director of the Three JI S Construction 
Company. 
Three JI S is both a source of income to Mr. Siino 
and he holds an investment in Three JI S within the meaning of 
§§87l03(a) and (c). 
See Government Code §§82030 and 82034. 
Accordingly, Mr. Siino may not make or participate in the 
making of any decisions that will foreseeably have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the pub-
lic generally, on ... Three JI S • 
In the instant situation it 
is undisputed that in November 1976 Three J's was the success-
ful bidder in a sealed bid situation on the construction of a 
building for PEHDC. 
However, approval of that project was 
considered by the Council prior to the time Three J's bid 
on the project. 
Since the decision to approve the project was made prior 
to the bid by Three JI S , the question becomes whether or not 
at the time the project was approved it was "foreseeable" that 
Three JI S would be the successful bidder on said project, and 
hence be benefitted financially by the decision. 
The Commis-
sion addressed a similar issue in an opinion requested by the 
Board of Directors of the Marin Municipal Water District, a 


Robert J. Logan 
December 5, 1977 
Page Three 
copy of which is enclosed (1 FPPC Ops. 198, No. 75-089, 
December 3, 1975). 
There, the question was whether Director 
MacPhail could vote on a project with which he had no known 
connection, although McPhails, Inc., may bid on the project 
or supply the project with certain materials. 
The Commis-
sion stated: 
On these facts alone, we cannot find a reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect on McPhail's. 
Mc-
Phail's has numerous competitors in each product 
it sells, except ready-mix conrete (for which 
there are three major competitors). 
The situa-
tion is unlike that of United States v. Missis-
sippi Valley Generating Company, supra., where 
"there was a substantial probability that, be-
cause of its prior experience in the area of pri-
vate power financing, First Boston would be hired 
to secure the financing ... ". 
364 U.S. at 555. 
Therefore, a material financial effect upon Mc-
Phail's, Inc. is not reasonably foreseeable and 
Director MacPhail's participation in the decision 
whether to grant a variance is not prohibited. 
In the instant case, it is our understanding that the 
Three J's Construction Company has not had previous dealings 
with PEHDC and that Three J's did not have a substantial edge 
over other competitors because of expertise, supplies or pre-
vious experience. 
From these facts, it does not appear that 
Mr. Siino acted in violation of the provisions of the Political 
Reform Act when he voted to approve the proposed project by 
PEHDC. 
I trust this answers your question with respect to the 
vote concerning the specific project in question. 
There 
remains, however, the prospective question of when Mr. Siino 
may continue to participate in matters involving PEHDC. 
The Act provides that the financial interests of an offi-
cial include a pro rata share of the investments, interests 
in real property and sources of income of any business entity 
in which the official owns a JO% or greater interest. 
Thus, 
if Mr. Siino owns 10% or more of the Three J' S Construction 
Company, the PEHDC will be a source of income to him if his 
pro rata share of income from that organization equals $250 
or more. 


Robert J. Logan 
December 5, 1977 
Page Four 
The question remaining is whether it is foreseeable 
that Mr. Siino will materially affect any of his financial 
interests when he participates in the decision of whether 
to approve or disapprove projects proposed by PEHDC. 
It is impossible for us to anticipate the nature of 
future decisions of the Council involving PEHDC. 
Further-
more, we have reason to believe from your letter that PEHDC 
may be a "local government agency" within the meaning of 
Government Code §8204l, and this might affect any conclu-
sions we would make concerning the effect of the decisions 
on PEHOC. 
Accordingly, we have enclosed a copy of the 
Commission's Opinion requested by Samuel Siegel, City 
Attorney of the City of Pico Rivera (3 FPPC Ops. 62, 
No. 76-054, July 6,1977). 
If future decisions of the 
Council involving PEHDC raise potential conflict of 
interest problems on Mr. Siino's part we would welcome 
your views concerning the applicability of the Siegel 
Opinion to the circumstances of PEHDC. 
We would also, 
in the interim, suggest that you be guided by the Tom 
Thorner Opinion, supra., and by the Commission's reg-
ulation defining "material financial effect", 2 Cal. 
Adm. Code §l8702, a copy of which is also enclosed. 
Thank you for writing. 
Please forgive our delay in 
responding. 
If we can be of any further assistance in 
this or any other matter, please feel free to calIon me 
at (916) 322-6444. 
KWG:glb 
enclosures 
Sincerely, 
ktvtAtXiJ~ t t l 
C~\.-O"1--/''-
Kenneth W. Goshorn 
Research Specialist III 
Conflicts of Interest Division 


OFFICE OF 
THE CITY ATTORNEY 
City of Pittsburg 
ROBERT J. LOGAN 
CITY ATTORNEY 
October 3, 1977 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Gentlemen: 
1901 RAILROAD AVENUE 
PITTSBURG, CALIFORNIA 94565 
(415)XM~ 
439-4890 
I am seeking an informal opinion of the Fair Political 
Practices Commission at the request of Joseph Siino, a City 
Councilmember in the City of Pittsburg, California. 
Mr. Siino is a principle in the Three J' s Cons·truction Company. 
In November of 1976, the Three J's Construction Company was 
the successful bidder in a sealed bid situation for the con-
struction of a building for Pittsburg Economic Housing and 
Development Corporation (PEHDC). 
PEHDC is funded primarily by 
City funds, though mostly federal, and operates as an economic 
and development corporation in the City. 
PEHDC is a non-profit corporation, duly organized under the 
laws of the State of California. 
Projects instituted by PEHDC 
in the City are sent to the City Council and proposed project 
concepts mayor may not be approved at the discretion of the 
Council, though no formal action is taken. 
Usually the Council 
merely indicates approval of a particular project. 
Upon 
approval of a project, PEHDC independently goes out to bid by 
sending out bid specifications which are then returned by pro-
spective contractors. 
For the particular project in question, there were six sealed 
bids SUbmitted. 
The bid of the Three J's Construction Company 
was the lowest and the contract was awarded to the Three J's. 
Except for the general approval or disapproval of project con-
ceptions, the Council does not directly act upon any contracts 
let by PEHDC. 
Furthermore, some projects of PEHDC are not 
even acted upon by the Council and the records are unclear 
whether this particular project ever came to the Council at all. 
If so, Mr. Siino may not have been an elected member of Council 
at the time. 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
October 3, 1977 
Page Two 
Mr. Siino was elected in March of 1976. 
It is very possible 
that the project conception may have been approved by the 
Council, but prior to the time of Mr. Siino's election. 
Un-
fortunately, the records are very unclear on this point. 
The mere approval of project conceptions by the Council in 
no way influences, affects or gives direction to PEHDC in 
conducting thereafter its own methods of preparing bid 
specifications and proceeding to bid. 
The determination of 
the acceptable bidder is not housed in the Council, but in 
the Board of Directors of PEHDC. 
Unfortunately, this particular problem was brought to my 
attention some six months after the bid had been let and the 
building was started, and at this point in time, the building 
is virtually completed. 
Councilmember Siino, however, desires an informal opinion from 
the FPPC, since several questions are still being asked by 
citizen groups within the City. 
Therefore, on behalf of 
Councilmember Siino, I respectfully request that the FPPC 
render an informal opinion on this matter. 
yours, 
RJL:dk

sections.question: Whether Mr. Joseph Siino, a City Councilmember in the City of Pittsburg and principal stockholder and director of Three J's Construction Company, violated the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act when he participated in a decision to approve a project proposed by the Pittsburg Economic Housing and Development Corporation (PEHDC), which was subsequently awarded to Three J's Construction Company in a sealed bid process in November 1976.
sections.conclusion: From the facts presented, it does not appear that Mr. Siino acted in violation of the provisions of the Political Reform Act when he voted to approve the proposed project by PEHDC. Since Three J's Construction Company had not had previous dealings with PEHDC and did not have a substantial edge over other competitors because of expertise, supplies or previous experience, a material financial effect on Three J's was not reasonably foreseeable at the time of the vote. The decision to approve the project was made prior to Three J's bid on the project, and the question of whether future decisions involving PEHDC may raise conflict of interest problems requires further analysis depending on the nature of those decisions and whether PEHDC is a local government agency.
sections.question_synthetic: Did Councilmember Siino violate conflict of interest provisions by voting to approve a PEHDC project when his company later bid on and won the construction contract, given that the project approval preceded the bidding process?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that Mr. Siino did not violate conflict of interest laws by voting to approve the PEHDC project because it was not reasonably foreseeable at the time of the vote that Three J's would benefit financially, given the company's lack of prior relationship with PEHDC and competitive position. Future PEHDC matters require evaluation based on specific circumstances and the agency's legal status.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (October 3, 1977 letter from City Attorney Logan) and FPPC response (December 5, 1977 letter from Kenneth W. Goshorn). Extracted information from the FPPC response letter. No formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION section headers, but clear factual recitation and analytical conclusion throughout. Minor OCR errors noted in header ('Califorqia', 'CommissioIl', 'Interes'). Confidence reduced slightly due to lack of explicit section headers and some ambiguity regarding PEHDC's legal status.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Mr. Joseph Siino, a City Councilmember in the City of Pittsburg and principal stockholder and director of Three J's Construction Company, violated the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act when he participated in a decision to approve a project proposed by the Pittsburg Economic Housing and Development Corporation (PEHDC), which was subsequently awarded to Three J's Construction Company in a sealed bid process in November 1976.

CONCLUSION: From the facts presented, it does not appear that Mr. Siino acted in violation of the provisions of the Political Reform Act when he voted to approve the proposed project by PEHDC. Since Three J's Construction Company had not had previous dealings with PEHDC and did not have a substantial edge over other competitors because of expertise, supplies or previous experience, a material financial effect on Three J's was not reasonably foreseeable at the time of the vote. The decision to approve the project was made prior to Three J's bid on the project, and the question of whether future decisions involving PEHDC may raise conflict of interest problems requires further analysis depending on the nature of those decisions and whether PEHDC is a local government agency.

embedding.summary: The FPPC addressed whether City Councilmember Siino violated conflict of interest law by voting to approve a PEHDC project that was later awarded to his construction company. The Commission concluded no violation occurred because the approval predated the bid and Three J's had no apparent advantage that would have made a financial benefit reasonably foreseeable at the time of the vote.
